one_line_takeaway: CCJA casts white-box LLM jailbreaking as gradient-based optimization of a hidden-state perturbation decoded through a local BERT proxy's MLM head, producing prefixes that beat GCG/AutoDAN/BEAST/COLD-Attack on attack success while achieving markedly lower perplexity and better SmoothLLM-defense resilience, and these prefixes transfer to boost black-box attacks (PAIR, PAP, CipherChat, ReNeLLM) against GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, and Claude-3.5.
key_finding: On the seven open-source LLMs (Table 1), CCJA achieves the best-or-second-best ASR/ASR-L in every case while consistently producing the lowest PPL among all methods (e.g., Vicuna7B: ASR 98.27, ASR-L 86.87, PPL 51.11 vs. GCG's ASR 96.92/PPL 224.19 and AutoDAN's ASR 97.63/PPL 87.48; Llama3: CCJA ASR 80.68/PPL 27.43 vs. AutoDAN ASR 80.23/PPL 71.14). On the safety-hardened Llama2-7B-chat, CCJA ranks second on ASR/ASR-L (67.17/61.72) but still has far lower PPL (86.32) than GCG's 422.96. Table 2 shows the LLM-generated initial prefix alone already achieves substantial ASR-i (e.g., 86.69 on Guanaco13B, 78.00 on Mistral-v0.2) with USE similarity to the optimized prefix exceeding 0.9, and optimization improves ASR over a semantically-void '!!!' baseline by over 20% on strongly-aligned models (Figure 2). Under SmoothLLM defense (Table 3), CCJA retains the highest ASR of the compared methods (e.g., Vicuna7B 89.27 vs. AutoDAN 29.68, BEAST 49.43, GCG 19.01), attributed to its semantic coherence resisting random token-level perturbation. In the multi-victim-model setting (Table 4), adding more target LLMs to a shared prefix slightly lowers direct ASR but raises cross-model transfer ASR (e.g., Llama3 transfer ASR rises from 47.29 with one target to 62.88 with three). Splicing CCJA-generated multi-LLM prefixes into black-box methods (Table 5) raises their ASR against closed-source models: e.g., PAIR's ASR on GPT-3.5 rises from 54.23 to 65.54 with CCJA, on GPT-4o from 43.10 to 54.01, on Claude-3.5 from 11.21 to 19.35; similar gains reported for PAP, CipherChat, and ReNeLLM across all three commercial targets.
core_contribution: CCJA (Context-Coherent Jailbreak Attack) is a white-box, token-level jailbreak attack for aligned open-source LLMs. It (1) uses the victim LLM itself, guided by a fixed seed prompt, to generate a natural-language 'instruction-following prefix' as the initialization for the jailbreak prefix (rather than a random suffix or hand-crafted template); (2) reframes the jailbreak search as continuous optimization of a perturbation delta in the hidden-state space of a local proxy masked language model (BERT-base-uncased), using the MLM's own decoder head H to decode perturbed hidden states back into tokens, with Gumbel-softmax for differentiable sampling and a vocabulary mapping matrix M to align MLM and victim-LLM token IDs; (3) optimizes a combined loss L = (1-beta)*L_jailbreak + beta*L_decode via gradient descent (Eq. 8-9), trading off attack success against fidelity to the natural-language initialization; and (4) extends this to multi-query/multi-victim-model settings via a voting mechanism (each victim LLM scores candidate prefixes) and a multi-objective loss summing per-model jailbreak losses. The paper also shows that jailbreak prefixes produced by CCJA can be spliced as seed prompts into existing black-box attack methods (PAIR, PAP, CipherChat, ReNeLLM) to boost their success against closed-source commercial LLMs.
threat_model: White-box attacker with full access to an open-source victim LLM's weights and gradients (and a separate local proxy MLM, BERT-base-uncased, used purely as an optimization/decoding vehicle -- not the victim model). Attacker crafts a jailbreak prefix J prepended to a malicious query Q from AdvBench, aiming to make the LLM produce an affirmative, harmful response R. A secondary, weaker threat model (Section 4.6) is transfer/black-box: prefixes optimized against open-source models are reused as seed text inside black-box attack pipelines (PAIR, PAP, CipherChat, ReNeLLM) targeting closed-source commercial LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, Claude-3.5), where the attacker only has query access to the target.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation is conducted solely on the AdvBench dataset for comparability with baselines, and the authors note harmful-instruction composition is growing more complex than AdvBench captures; (2) the method is designed for white-box (open-source) targets and 'may not be applicable in black-box scenarios' directly -- the black-box gains in Section 4.6 rely on splicing CCJA prefixes into other black-box frameworks (PAIR/PAP/CipherChat/ReNeLLM) rather than an inherent black-box capability of CCJA itself; (3) for highly safety-aligned models (Llama2, Llama3) the method still follows the established pattern of appending affirmative-response cues, an approach the authors say they intend to move beyond with fully conversational-template attacks in future work.

quantitative_results: Table 1 ASR/ASR-L/PPL on Llama2-7B-chat: CCJA 67.17/61.72/86.32 vs GCG 75.61/65.23/422.96 (CCJA ranks second on ASR/ASR-L but lowest PPL among close performers)
Table 1 ASR/ASR-L/PPL on Llama3-8B-Instruct: CCJA 80.68/73.69/27.43 vs AutoDAN 80.23/70.01/71.14 vs GCG 59.12/51.21/124.55
Table 2 initial-prefix-only ASR-i (before optimization) ranges from 15.60 (Llama2) to 86.69 (Guanaco13B); USE similarity between initial and optimized prefixes exceeds 0.9 for weakly-aligned models
Figure 2: optimization improves ASR over a semantically-void '!!!'-initialized baseline by over 20% for strongly safety-aligned victim LLMs
Table 3 (SmoothLLM defense) ASR on Vicuna7B: CCJA 89.27 vs AutoDAN 29.68 vs BEAST 49.43 vs GCG 19.01; on Llama3: CCJA 79.72 vs AutoDAN 75.49 vs BEAST 62.36 vs GCG 5.23

Table 4 multi-victim transfer: Llama3 cross-model transfer ASR rises as more target LLMs are added to the joint optimization (47.29 with Mistral-v0.3 alone, 51.68 adding Vicuna7B, 62.88 adding Llama3), while direct ASR on the primary targets (e.g., Mistral-v0.3 95.59 -> 94.05 -> 93.26) decreases slightly
baselines_compared: SmoothLLM (Robey et al., 2023) as a defense baseline
PPL-threshold filtering as a defense baseline
